Figure 1 This chart illustrates the uneven distribution of the target variable y: the proportion of non-subscribed clients reaches 88.8%, while the proportion of successfully converted clients is only 11.2%. Due to this distribution characteristic, our subsequent logistic regression model may exhibit certain bias during the fitting process. Therefore, when evaluating the model’s performance, we do not solely rely on the overall accuracy rate (because even if the model predicts all results as “no”, the final accuracy rate can still exceed 80%). On the other hand, we will pay more attention to the model’s ability to identify these 11.21% key samples, so that the final prediction results have practical reference value.

By combining the correlation matrix Figure 2 and Principal Component Analysis Table 1, it can be observed that euribor3m, nr.employed, and emp.var.rate have a high degree of positive correlation (the pairwise correlation coefficients are all greater than 0.9), and multiple collinearity can also be seen in the first principal component (Comp.1). For the subsequent modeling performance, we decided to exclude nr.employed and emp.var.rate and retain euribor3m. Because euribor3m represents the Euro Interbank Offered Rate, it can better reflect the macro-financial situation during bank marketing, and can relatively better help us explain the final subscription situation of clients.

Since the variable default only has two values, “no” and “unknown”, and over 78% of the cases are “no” with no “yes”, the available information is extremely limited. Therefore, we have decided to remove this variable.

Figure 3 This set of graphs respectively shows the relationships between several key categorical variables and the variable indicating whether the client subscribes (y). From the graphs, it can be seen that the previous marketing outcome (poutcome) obviously affects whether the client subscribes this time. Clients who were successfully marketed before (success) have a higher probability of subscribing than those who were marketing failed (failure) or had no previous contact (nonexistent). Moreover, there are certain differences between different occupations (job) and educational levels (education), indicating that the social characteristics of the clients may have an impact on the subscription decision. At the same time, there are also certain differences in contact methods (contact), with clients contacted via mobile phone (cellular) having a slightly higher chance of subscribing than those contacted via telephone (telephone).

Overall, historical marketing results, client social characteristics, and communication channels may all have an impact on whether the client subscribes this time. Therefore, these variables will have certain predictive value in the subsequent modeling process.

Divide the dataset into a training set and a test set in a ratio of 8:2.

3 Formal Analysis

##Decision Tree ###Model Description and Justification A decision tree classifier was applied as the first tree-based method. Decision trees recursively partition the feature space by selecting the variable and split point that best separates the classes at each node, measured by the Gini impurity criterion. The resulting model is fully interpretable, as its predictions can be explained through a sequence of human-readable if-then rules. This is especially useful in a banking context, where understanding why a client is predicted to subscribe is of clear practical importance.

Given the substantial class imbalance in the dataset (88.8% “no”), equal class priors were assigned (prior = c(0.5, 0.5)) to prevent the tree from trivially favouring the majority class. Without this adjustment, a tree predicting “no” for every observation would achieve over 88% accuracy while providing no useful predictive information.

3.0.1 Model fitting and pruning

A full unpruned tree was first grown with the complexity parameter cp = 0, allowing the tree to expand until no further splits were possible. Unpruned trees are prone to overfitting — they learn noise specific to the training data and generalise poorly to unseen observations. Pruning is therefore necessary to find the right balance between bias and variance. The optimal cp was selected using the 1-SE rule: rather than choosing the cp corresponding to the absolute minimum cross-validation error (which tends to favour overly complex trees), the largest cp whose cross-validation error falls within one standard error of the minimum was selected. As shown in Figure 4, the cross-validation error curve flattens quickly after approximately 35 nodes, indicating that additional complexity yields negligible improvement. The 1-SE rule selected a tree with only 3 leaf nodes, which is both parsimonious and less prone to overfitting.

3.0.2 Tree structure and interpretation

The pruned tree (Figure 5) identifies two variables as the most informative predictors: First split — euribor3m >= 1.3: The Euribor 3-month rate, a macro-economic indicator reflecting the prevailing interest rate environment, forms the root split. When euribor3m < 1.3 (low interest rate environment), the tree predicts “yes” with 83% probability, covering 14% of the test data. This is economically intuitive: when market interest rates are low, term deposits offer relatively more attractive returns, increasing clients’ willingness to subscribe. Second split — month: Among observations where euribor3m >= 1.3, the contact month further differentiates clients. Contacts made in April, March, and October yield a 68% subscription rate, compared to 31% in the remaining months. This may reflect seasonal patterns in clients’ financial planning or campaign timing effects. The simplicity of the final tree with two splits and three rules is both a strength and a limitation. It is highly interpretable and robust to overfitting, but it necessarily ignores the predictive information contained in the remaining variables.

3.0.3 Model evaluation

Performance on the test set is summarised below in Table 3.

Accuracy is a misleading metric here due to the class imbalance in the dataset. A classifier that predicts “no” for every observation would achieve 88.6% accuracy, the No Information Rate, yet the decision tree’s accuracy of 80.95% is lower. This is expected: by assigning equal class priors, the model deliberately trades majority-class accuracy for a better ability to detect true subscribers.

Sensitivity and AUC are more meaningful in this context. A Sensitivity of 0.597 means the model correctly identifies around 60% of clients who would actually subscribe, compared to only 22.8% under the unweighted tree. The AUC of 0.724 indicates moderate discriminative ability, considerably better than random classification (AUC = 0.5). The Balanced Accuracy of 0.717 further confirms that performance is reasonable across both classes.

The Positive Predictive Value of 0.320 means that among clients predicted to subscribe, about 32% would do so. Given the base subscription rate of 11.4%, this still represents a substantial improvement in targeting efficiency over random outreach.

3.1 Random Forest


Call:
 randomForest(formula = y ~ ., data = train_data, ntree = 500,      importance = TRUE) 
               Type of random forest: classification
                     Number of trees: 500
No. of variables tried at each split: 4

        OOB estimate of  error rate: 9.8%
Confusion matrix:
      no yes class.error
no  6935 172  0.02420149
yes  612 281  0.68533035

3.1.1 Model description and justification

Random Forest is an ensemble learning method that constructs a large number of decision trees, each trained on a bootstrap sample of the training data. At each split, only a randomly selected subset of features is considered, which decorrelates the trees and reduces the variance of the ensemble compared to a single decision tree. Predictions are made by majority vote across all trees. This combination of bagging and feature randomisation makes Random Forest particularly robust to overfitting and well suited to tabular classification problems with mixed variable types.

mtry = 4  OOB error = 9.9% 
Searching left ...

0.009067358 0.01

2.OOB    2 0.095625

4.OOB    4 0.099000
6.OOB    6 0.099625

3.1.2 Hyperparameter tuning

The key hyperparameter in Random Forest is mtry, the number of variables randomly considered at each split. The default value in R for classification is \(\lfloor\sqrt{p}\rfloor\), where \(p\) is the number of predictors, giving mtry = 4 in this case. To find the optimal value, tuneRF was applied, evaluating each candidate using Out-of-Bag (OOB) error on the training data. As shown in Figure 6, OOB error is minimised at mtry = 2 (OOB error = 9.56%), with error increasing as mtry grows.

Optimal threshold: 0.083

3.1.3 Handling class imbalance

The default classification threshold of 0.5 is poorly suited to imbalanced data, as it systematically under-predicts the minority class. Adjusting class weights was explored but found to reduce AUC by distorting the model’s probability estimates. Instead, the threshold was optimised after fitting using Youden’s J statistic, which identifies the value that maximises the sum of Sensitivity and Specificity simultaneously. The optimal threshold was 0.083, which reflects the low base rate of subscription (11.4%) in the dataset.

3.1.4 Variable importance

Figure 7 presents variable importance under two measures. Mean Decrease in Accuracy captures how much predictive performance drops when a variable’s values are randomly permuted, providing a direct measure of its contribution to model accuracy. Mean Decrease in Gini reflects the total reduction in node impurity attributable to each variable across all trees.

Both measures consistently rank euribor3m as the most important predictor, reinforcing the finding from the decision tree that the prevailing interest rate environment is the strongest driver of subscription behaviour. month and age rank highly under Mean Decrease in Accuracy, while age and job are prominent under Mean Decrease in Gini. The two measures do not always agree because they capture different aspects of importance: permutation accuracy is more reliable when predictors are correlated, while Gini importance can favour variables with many distinct values. Taken together, the results suggest that macro-economic conditions, client demographics, and campaign timing are the most informative predictors. Variables such as loan, housing, and contact rank near the bottom on both measures, indicating limited independent predictive value once stronger predictors are accounted for.

3.1.5 Model evaluation

Performance on the test set at the optimal threshold is summarised in Table 4.

The Random Forest outperforms the single decision tree across all key metrics. An AUC of 0.789 indicates that the model correctly ranks a randomly chosen subscriber above a randomly chosen non-subscriber approximately 79% of the time. Sensitivity of 0.636 means that roughly two thirds of true subscribers are correctly identified, an improvement over the decision tree (0.597). Specificity remains high at 0.867, confirming that this gain is not achieved by predicting “yes” indiscriminately. The Balanced Accuracy of 0.751 reflects a reasonable trade-off between the two classes given the degree of imbalance present.

The Positive Predictive Value of 0.381 indicates that among clients flagged as likely subscribers, 38% would actually subscribe. This is more than three times the base subscription rate of 11.4%, meaning the model would considerably improve the efficiency of targeted outreach compared to contacting clients at random.

4 Conclusions
